data.title: LPL forecasts 2026 core G&A at $2.155B-$2.19B as it plans $125M Q2 buybacks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LPL Financial (LPLA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO Richard Steinmeier said Q1 was “a strong start to the year” with “solid organic asset growth,” progress “to onboard Commonwealth Financial Network,” and “meaningful progress driving improved operating leverage,” while noting “rising macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty” and “an increasingly loud and often speculative narrative around the role of artificial intelligence and wealth management.”
  • Steinmeier reported that “total assets decreased to $2.3 trillion” and the firm “attracted organic net new assets of $21 billion,” and added that “recruited assets improved to $17 billion,” with the “overall pipeline” pushed “to record levels.”
  • On Commonwealth, Steinmeier said “asset retention… is in the mid-80s today” and “we continue to track towards our target of 90% retention,” while emphasizing preserving “Commonwealth’s indispensable approach to adviser satisfaction” and building “a comprehensive case management solution” to modernize service and operations.
  • CFO Matthew Audette said the quarter reflected “strong first quarter business and financial performance,” highlighting “adjusted pretax margin of approximately 38% and record adjusted EPS of $5.60,” and added that LPL “opportunistically resumed buybacks earlier this month with roughly $125 million planned for Q2.”

Outlook

  • Audette guided to 2026 core G&A of “$2.155 billion to $2.19 billion,” stating the company is “lowering the upper end of our outlook range by $20 million,” and guided Q2 core G&A to “$540 million to $560 million.”
  • Audette said, “we expect our payout rate will increase approximately 50 basis points in Q2,” and that “based on where client cash balances and interest rates are today, we expect our ICA yield to be roughly flat” in Q2.
  • Audette guided Q2 service and fee revenue to “increase by approximately $5 million,” Q2 transaction revenue to “decline by roughly $5 million,” and Q2 TA loan amortization to “increase by roughly $10 million.”
  • Compared with Q4’s buyback stance (“we paused buybacks… with a plan to revisit following the onboard”), Q1 included an explicit restart: Audette said LPL “resumed buybacks” with “roughly $125 million planned for Q2.”

Financial results

  • Adjusted EPS was $5.60 vs. analysts’ $5.47.
  • Steinmeier said “total assets decreased to $2.3 trillion” as “organic growth was more than offset by lower equity markets,” and Audette said total client assets were “down slightly from Q4.”
  • Audette reported gross profit of “$1.593 billion,” and said commission and advisory fees net of payout were “$487 million,” with a payout rate of “87.2%.”
  • Audette reported client cash revenue of “$460 million” and ending client cash balances of “$59 billion,” adding that within ICA, “the mix of fixed rate balances ended the quarter at roughly 60%,” and ICA yield was “336 basis points.”
  • On Commonwealth economics, Audette said LPL “now estimate[s] run rate EBITDA of approximately $410 million once fully integrated,” and attributed the change to “market-driven decline in Q1 assets.”

Q&A

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: asked about “structural headwinds to cash flow” from “Agentic AI tools” and “a more fee-based model”; CEO Steinmeier responded, “we don’t see an imminent risk,” but “we’re doing the work… to properly assess the opportunities and risks of reducing our reliance on cash sweep economics over time.”
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked about “incremental M&A” vs. repurchases; CFO Audette said near-term focus is “integrating Commonwealth” plus “clear and compelling returns on buying back our stock,” and Steinmeier added LPL will remain disciplined across “growing our markets,” “liquidity and succession,” and “capability transactions.”
  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: asked why adviser count fell while retention was strong; Audette said it was “a near-term dynamic with respect to Commonwealth” as departures flow through headcount.
  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP: asked how AI affects productivity and consolidation; Steinmeier said LPL views AI as “an incredibly powerful tool… and not as a replacement,” outlining use cases in adviser tools, “straight-through processing,” and faster software development.
  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan: asked about recruiting momentum and April; Steinmeier said the pipeline is “at record levels,” while Audette said April cash reflected “advisory fees… around $2.5 billion” and taxes reducing cash “about $3 billion,” and that April organic growth would be “around 1.5%.”
  • Christopher Allen, KBW: asked about lower Commonwealth EBITDA run rate; Audette said the move from “$425 million to $410 million” was “all market-driven,” and if markets hold, “I would expect us to be back at $425 million.”
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: asked if anything “under the surface” impacted EBITDA; Audette said, “No, not at all… No changes to expected synergies.”
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: pressed on why AI won’t increase cash sorting and what “work” entails; Steinmeier said cash sorting “has already happened” and cash is “around $5,000 per account,” while Audette said LPL has “straightforward fee-based levers.”
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: asked about Commonwealth synergy drivers; Audette cited custody/clearing revenue items “whether it be cash sweep” and “sponsor-related revenues,” plus “typical expense synergies.”
  • Michael Brown, UBS: asked if AI platforms threaten the adviser model; Steinmeier emphasized “trust and extended relationship” and said AI can “commoditize” low-value tasks while enhancing personalization.
  • Wilma Jackson Burdis, Raymond James: asked payout drivers; Audette said, “It was Commonwealth,” citing larger AUM advisers and “noise that’s unique to Q1.”
  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan (follow-up): asked where efficiency gains came from; Audette pointed to AI in service/operations and said certain processes could see “90%” cycle-time reduction.
  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research (follow-up): asked about long-term organic growth and enterprise pipeline; Steinmeier said LPL believes it can sustain “mid- to high single-digit growth rate over the long term,” and cited institutional momentum, including Prudential “thriving.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to skeptical, concentrating on cash monetization durability and AI disruption risk, including “structural headwinds to cash flow” (Chubak) and “AI disruption risk” (Brown).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and explanatory; Steinmeier said, “we don’t see an imminent risk,” and framed AI as “an extension and continuation of our strategy,” while Audette repeatedly emphasized “no changes to expected synergies” on Commonwealth.
  • Versus Q4’s focus on Commonwealth retention headlines, Q1’s questioning broadened to AI/cash economics and capital allocation, while management shifted from “plan to revisit” buybacks to having “resumed buybacks.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1, Steinmeier said assets “decreased to $2.3 trillion,” versus Q4’s “record $2.4 trillion.”
  • Q1 adjusted EPS was “$5.60,” versus Q4’s “$5.23.”
  • Q1 organic net new assets were “$21 billion,” versus Q4’s “$23 billion.”
  • Commonwealth EBITDA language changed from Q4 “approximately $425 million” to Q1 “approximately $410 million,” with Audette stating the shift was “all market-driven” and reversible if markets hold.
  • Capital return language tightened from Q4’s potential timing refinement to Q1’s explicit plan: “roughly $125 million planned for Q2.”

Risks and concerns

  • Steinmeier highlighted “macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty” and addressed AI uncertainty as an “often speculative narrative.”
  • Management acknowledged ongoing sensitivity to cash economics; Steinmeier said LPL is assessing “reducing our reliance on cash sweep economics over time,” while emphasizing it must be a “fair value exchange.”
  • Integration execution remains central; Audette said Commonwealth onboarding remains “on track to onboard in the fourth quarter,” with retention “in the mid-80s today” against a “target of 90% retention.”

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a strong start, anchored by $21B of organic net new assets, record adjusted EPS of $5.60, and improved operating leverage, while preparing to onboard Commonwealth in Q4 with mid-80% asset retention tracking toward a 90% target. Executives addressed investor focus on AI and cash monetization by emphasizing existing cash-sorting behavior and “fee-based levers,” and they signaled a notable capital allocation shift by resuming buybacks with roughly $125M planned for Q2 alongside a narrowed 2026 core G&A outlook range of $2.155B to $2.19B.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rivian revises DOE loan down to $4.5B for Georgia factory
data.description:
Rivian (RIVN) has revised its loan agreement with the U.S. Department of Energy, now planning to draw up to $4.5B to fund construction of its Georgia factory, reduced from the $6.6B initially allocated during the Biden administration.

The company said Thursday it now plans to begin drawing on its loan in early 2027, earlier than previously expected, while increasing the Georgia plant’s initial production capacity to 300,000 vehicles annually from 200,000.

The 50% boost in first-phase capacity is aimed at lowering per-unit costs and strengthening operating leverage while also leaving room for further expansion in subsequent phases, the company said.

The company added that production of its upcoming R2 model is still slated to begin in late 2028 at the Georgia facility, following the recent start of production at its existing plant in Normal, Illinois.

CEO RJ Scaringe told CNBC’s Phil LeBeau that any future expansion of the Georgia plant would be funded internally, as Rivian continues to raise capital through partnerships with companies including Volkswagen and Uber.

The revised loan details were announced alongside Rivian’s Q1 results. Consolidated revenue was up 11.3% year over year to $1.38B, but loss from operations amounted to $881M compared to $655M a year ago, due to lower gross profit and higher operating expenses.

Rivian (RIVN) produced 10,236 vehicles at its manufacturing facility in Normal, Illinois, during the quarter and delivered 10,365 vehicles during the same period. By comparison, Rivian (RIVN) produced 14,611 vehicles and delivered 8,640 vehicles in Q1 of 2025.

data.title: Blue Owl sold half of SpaceX's stake at a $1.25T valuation
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital (OWL) co-CEO Marc Lipschultz said that the company has sold about half of its stake in SpaceX (SPACE) at a $1.25T valuation, crystallizing roughly a tenfold return.

"We made about 10x our money," and "we’ve sold about half of it at a $1.25 trillion valuation." Lipschultz said on an earnings call, still holding about half of it.

Blue Owl (OWL) was among SpaceX’s earliest lenders and later converted its exposure into an equity investment, Lipschultz said. "We made a loan to the company and had ⁠the ​privilege of getting to know them very well and ​then participating in ongoing conversations about other financing opportunities," he added.

The initial investment was made in 2021 through Blue Owl Technology Finance (OTF), with ~$27M deployed. The position had grown to about $195M in fair value by the end of 2025.

Separately, another Blue Owl-managed vehicle, Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), also held a smaller stake in SpaceX valued at about $21.7M at the end of 2025.

Lipschultz added that, in case of credit losses, sales of this kind "are the ways ... we ​can offset some of those losses."

data.title: MPS raises enterprise data growth floor to ~85% while targeting $6B capacity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “In Q1, MPS achieved record quarterly revenue of $804 million, 7% higher than the fourth quarter of 2025 and 26% higher than the first quarter of 2025,” said Tony Balow.
  • Balow highlighted demand drivers and strategic expansion: “Our communications end market grew 33% sequentially on the strength of our power solutions for optical modules and switches,” and “MPS continued to grow our capacity past our original $4 billion plan with a new goal of reaching $6 billion in the near future.”
  • On product expansion, Balow said, “We sampled our first high-speed interface products for DDR5 at major customers,” and positioned the longer-term shift: “MPS…accelerate our transition from chips only to a full-service silicon-based solution provider.”
  • CEO Michael R. Hsing emphasized competitive differentiation and execution: “We are the best in the market segment because we provide a total monolithic power solutions,” and “we don't want to be the dominant suppliers…our goal is to diversify growth.”

Outlook

  • Management raised its Enterprise Data growth framing vs. last quarter: “In the last call, we kind of rose that to a 50% floor…at this point in time, I think we're comfortable raising that floor up to around 85% year-over-year growth,” said Tony Balow.
  • On gross margin direction and caution, Balow said, “For Q2…we did have the confidence to increase incrementally our gross margins…[but] we do…see some strong headwinds potentially in the second half. And so we're not…remaining cautious for the guidance in the second half of the year.” (No specific Q2 revenue, EPS, or gross margin percentage guidance was stated in the transcript.)
  • On end-market pacing, Balow reiterated: “Auto…would be roughly flat for the first half of the year and ramping in later in the year,” and for Storage & Compute, “we're still very optimistic on storage…more cautious on notebook.”

Financial results

  • The company reported “record quarterly revenue of $804 million,” and tied the upside to mix and end-market strength, including “communications…33% sequentially.”
  • On profitability framework, Balow said, “For the last 4 quarters, we've been flat at 55.5%,” describing it as “at the low end of our gross margin model…55…to upper 50s,” while Hsing added, “I don't want to give you a false hope that we're going to jump very high.”
  • On pricing/cost actions to protect profitability, Hsing said, “some of the cost is higher…so we will keep…the goal is that we're keeping our margin profiles,” and Balow added, “in those cases…you could see us raise prices to stay within our gross margin model.”

Q&A

  • Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank: Asked about “XPU…versus…CPU” demand and storage/computing tailwinds; Tony Balow: “CPU being a tailwind…we continue to see that here in 2026,” and on Storage & Compute, “very optimistic about storage staying strong…much more cautious on the notebook side.”
  • William Stein, Truist: Asked about capacity geography and “latest BCD generation”; CEO Michael R. Hsing: “We are still around 60 nanometers and maybe we'll go down to 40, 45 nanometers,” and Tony Balow: “very geographically diverse, both inside and outside of China.”
  • Joshua Buchalter, TD Cowen: Asked for segment guideposts and CPU vs AI upside split; CEO Hsing: “I'm not going to give it to you,” while Tony Balow: “we're comfortable raising that floor up to around 85% year-over-year growth…[and] we don't want to try to parse out between volume and content.”
  • Richard Schafer, Oppenheimer: Asked if supply limits upside and about robotics timing; CEO Hsing: “MPS is one of the players,” and on robotics, “We see this year…volume are still low…very difficult to predict,” while Balow said MPS is “trying to engage broadly and win all the designs we can.”
  • Quinn Bolton, Needham: Asked if comms could outgrow Enterprise Data and about 800V plus GaN/SiC; CEO Hsing: “we're not in the business to predicting…market trend,” and “we based on silicon carbide,” adding, “we developed our GaN…not for 800 volts…for lower voltage.”
  • Tore Svanberg, Stifel: Asked about 2,000W GPUs differentiation and DDR5 interface revenue timing; CEO Hsing cited “monolithics…integrations…modules” and “fully automated” test/reliability, while Tony Balow: “I wouldn't really have that as being a contributor to 2026 revenue.”
  • Gary Mobley, Loop: Asked comms content, channel inventory, and pricing; Tony Balow: “we're not going to talk about specific content,” “the channel has been very lean,” and on pricing, “there are places where input costs have gotten higher…[and]…raise prices.”
  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: Asked why revenue acceleration doesn’t translate to big GM move and about robotics content; Tony Balow: “headwinds potentially in the second half,” and CEO Hsing: “for the dollar content…it's very difficult to say.”
  • Christopher Caso, Wolfe: Asked Merchant vs ASIC in Enterprise Data and auto visibility; CEO Hsing: “we don't know how to separate it,” and Tony Balow said auto ramp expectations are tied to “designs that we previously won coming to market.”
  • Wei Chia, Citi: Asked SiC vs GaN rationale and supply-chain readiness; CEO Hsing: “I still don't believe for high power,” and described inventory strategy: “we have actively preemptively…build these inventory,” while Tony Balow: “the 85% floor was [not] limited by something, that's not an issue right now.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with repeated emphasis on upside capture and sustainability, including Schafer’s “wow…on the outlook” and multiple questions probing constraints, content, and gross margin durability.
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks (record revenue, comms strength, $6B capacity goal), and more guarded/deflective in Q&A on content splits, with CEO Hsing stating, “I'm not going to give it to you,” and Balow repeatedly declining to “parse out between volume and content.”
  • Versus last quarter, the tone shifted toward higher confidence in Enterprise Data visibility (floor raised), while maintaining caution on second-half margin headwinds.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Enterprise Data framing increased from Q4’s “a floor of 50%” (Executive VP & CFO Bernie Blegen) to Q1’s “around 85% year-over-year growth” (Tony Balow).
  • Manufacturing capacity messaging advanced from Q4’s “more than $4 billion of geographically balanced capacity” (Balow) to Q1’s “new goal of reaching $6 billion in the near future” (Balow).
  • Leadership context changed from Q4’s CFO transition (“Bernie will be retiring…Rob Dean…interim CFO”) to Q1 execution under “Rob Dean, Interim CFO,” with prepared remarks led by Balow.
  • Analyst focus remained heavily concentrated on Enterprise Data/AI power, comms (optical modules/switches), and 800V architectures; Q1 added more explicit probing on pricing, gross margin cadence, and robotics monetization.

Risks and concerns

  • Geopolitics and macro uncertainty remained a stated overhang: “We continue to adjust to the fluid geopolitical and macroeconomic environment,” said Tony Balow.
  • Margin risk was concentrated in timing and cost pressures: Balow warned of “strong headwinds potentially in the second half,” and noted expedited supply-chain costs may require pricing actions.
  • Demand variability risk was explicit in notebooks and customer ramps: Balow said MPS is “more cautious on the notebook side,” while both Hsing and Balow repeated that MPS “can't control when the customers ramp.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 around record $804 million revenue, a sharp sequential jump in communications, and a larger manufacturing ambition (capacity goal raised to $6 billion). The most material update versus last quarter was the Enterprise Data outlook language, with management lifting its year-over-year growth “floor” to ~85% based on extended ordering patterns and backlog visibility, while simultaneously signaling second-half gross margin headwinds and continued reluctance to quantify content by platform or customer.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Asian markets navigate record Australian inflation and surging Japan factory data
Trump says Hormuz blockade is 'incredible,' even as gas prices touch all-time high
data.description:
The U.S. and Iran continue to lock horns in a prolonged standoff, with President Donald Trump reaffirming his commitment to a naval blockade of Iranian ports despite surging oil prices and global economic concerns.

Trump said the blockade is effectively “choking” Iran’s economy and signaled it would remain in place until Tehran agrees to curb its nuclear program.

“Their economy is crashing, the blockade is incredible,” Trump told reporters at the White House. “Their economy is a disaster. So we’ll see how long they hold out.”

“The blockade is somewhat more effective than the bombing,” Trump had previously made comments to Axios. “They are choking like a stuffed pig.”

The oil markets have remained volatile as traders weigh the risk of prolonged supply disruptions. U.S. fuel prices are climbing rapidly, with the national average hitting fresh highs and retail gasoline in California topping $6 per gallon, a Bloomberg report said. This spike could be a worry for the Republican Party as midterm elections approach.

“The gas will go down,” Trump said. “As soon as the war is over, it’ll drop like a rock.”

Separately, White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett told reporters on Thursday, "If you look at the economic stress that the Iranian people are under right now, it should be unacceptable to any civilized leader."

Iran “is soon nearing storage capacity, which will force the regime to reduce oil production, resulting in an additional approximately $170 million per day in lost revenue, and causing permanent damage to Iran’s oil infrastructure.” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent wrote on X.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Asure outlines 2026 revenue of $159M-$163M with 23%-25% adjusted EBITDA margin as Asure Central adoption expands
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Asure Software (ASUR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “First quarter revenues came in at $42.8 million, representing growth of 23% compared to Q1 of 2025.” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel)

  • “Our organic growth rate for quarter 1 2026 was 7% compared with 3% in quarter 1 2025, and 3.5% in quarter 1 2024.” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel)

  • “Since the launch of Asure Central in October 2025, adoption has continued at a rapid pace, and we believe that by the end of the second quarter of 2026, the majority of our approximately 30,000 direct clients will be on the platform.” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel)

  • “Earlier this year, at our sales kickoff, we introduced AsureWorks, which is our administrative services outsourcing or ASO model… Clients who adopt managed payroll and compliance services typically represent 2x to 3x the revenue of a payroll-only client.” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel)

  • “First quarter total revenues were $42.8 million compared to $34.9 million in Q1 2025, representing growth of 23% year-over-year.” (CFO John Pence)

Outlook

  • “Based on continued positive momentum in our business, we are updating our full year 2026 guidance and also providing Q2 guidance.” (CFO John Pence)

  • “Full year 2026 guidance. Revenue of $159 million to $163 million, and adjusted EBITDA margin of 23% to 25%.” (CFO John Pence)

  • “Q2 2026 guidance. Revenue of $36 million to $38 million, and adjusted EBITDA of $6 million to $8 million.” (CFO John Pence)

  • “It's important to keep in mind that the first quarters are seasonally strong as recurring year-end W2/ACA revenue is recognized in this period.” (CFO John Pence)

Financial Results

  • “Recurring revenue for Q1 2026 was $37.8 million… Recurring revenue represented approximately 88% of total revenue in the quarter.” (CFO John Pence)

  • “Professional services and hardware revenue was $5 million in Q1 2026… The increase in nonrecurring revenue was primarily due to hardware sales from our Lathem acquisition, and professional services tied to enterprise tax.” (CFO John Pence)

  • “Net income for Q1 2026 was $0.6 million compared to a net loss of $2.4 million in Q1 of 2025.” (CFO John Pence)

  • “Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026 was $12.3 million… Adjusted EBITDA margin for Q1 2026 was 29% compared to 21% in Q1 of 2025.” (CFO John Pence)

  • “We ended the first quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $19.2 million and debt of $68.8 million as of March 31, 2026.” (CFO John Pence)

Q&A

  • Jeff Van Rhee, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: “On Asure Central… what are you observing with respect to the path of adoption…?” Chairman & CEO Goepel: “Attach rates were up about 15% year-over-year… we're really, really pleased.”

  • Jeff Van Rhee, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: “Where… are you at this point in terms of PEPM…?” Chairman & CEO Goepel: “We were kind of in the area of $12 to $15 per employee per month… I would be disappointed… that we don't do a double over the next 2 to 3 years here.”

  • Joshua Reilly, Needham & Company, LLC: “The 7% organic growth is pretty impressive… How much… was the forms growth…?” Chairman & CEO Goepel: “That was the forms growth… there was no headwind in forms growth. Maybe it's 1%.”

  • Joshua Reilly, Needham & Company, LLC: “On the Lathem transition… how is that going?” CFO Pence: “We’re going to be in earnest in the back half of this year and into next converting to that HaaS model… over the next… 18 months to 2 years.”

  • Jared Levine, TD Cowen: “What do you see in terms of the revenue opportunity… from those managed service offerings?” Chairman & CEO Goepel: “We see an opportunity of about $50 or so per employee per month where we're doing the work for them… I do think you'll see somewhere around kind of $3 million to $5 million in opportunity in this year's revenue.”

  • Richard Baldry, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC: “When you talk about accelerating to double-digit organic growth… about the pieces that get you there?” Chairman & CEO Goepel: “The ARPU, the attach rate, the number of reps, the rollout of Asure Central, the rollout of AsureWorks… really good pipeline development.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • “So it looks like you didn't pass through all of the quarterly revenue and adjusted EBITDA beat. Anything to call out there?” (Jared Levine, TD Cowen) and “we are… 10 under where I really would like to be” on sales hiring (Chairman & CEO Goepel) reflected slightly negative analyst scrutiny alongside management’s measured tone on execution.

  • “We are very pleased to report a strong start in 2026” (Chairman & CEO Goepel) and “Based on continued positive momentum in our business, we are updating our full year 2026 guidance” (CFO Pence) reflected positive prepared-remarks sentiment.

  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on “we believe that we are at an inflection point” and announcing an AI fireside chat (Chairman & CEO Goepel, Q4 2025), Q1 2026 carried a more execution-focused tone around platform adoption, attach rates, and guidance specificity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 guidance expanded the annual revenue range to “$159 million to $163 million” (CFO John Pence) versus Q4 2025’s “between $159 million and $162 million” (CFO John Pence).

  • Asure Central adoption language moved from “more than 2/3 of our clients upgrading” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel, Q4 2025) to “by the end of the second quarter of 2026, the majority of our approximately 30,000 direct clients will be on the platform” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel, Q1 2026).

  • Q4 2025 highlighted “a very healthy contracted backlog of approximately $100 million” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel), while Q1 2026 reported “contracted backlog remains healthy at approximately $85.6 million” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel).

  • Analysts stayed centered on attach rates, Asure Central adoption, AI impact, and Lathem/HaaS mix shift across both calls, with Q1 adding more detailed questions on AsureWorks economics and implied conservatism in guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • “Given global uncertainty, we're taking a conservative stance on operating the business.” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel)

  • “We have modeled 2 additional rate cuts in 2026, which we anticipate will be partially offset by continued growth in client fund balances.” (CFO John Pence)

  • “We won't cross sell without their permission into those kind of companies that we have relationships with.” (Chairman & CEO Patrick Goepel)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as a start to a year where platform consolidation and service expansion are expected to compound: Asure Central nearing broad migration, Luna AI showing higher usage and operational deployment, and AsureWorks positioned as a higher-revenue service layer without co-employment risk, alongside raised full-year revenue range guidance and reaffirmed profitability targets tied to adjusted EBITDA margins.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NatWest GAAP EPS of 17.80p, total income of £4.36B; updates FY26 outlook
data.description:
• NatWest press release (NWG): Q1 GAAP EPS of 17.80p.
  • Total income of £4.36B (+9.5% Y/Y).
  • Return on Tangible Equity of 18.2% drove strong capital generation pre-distributions of 65 basis points in the quarter and further growth in TNAV per share, up 16 pence to 400 pence.
  • Outlook: Based on our latest expectations for interest rates and economic conditions, we now expect income excluding notable items to be at the top end of our previously guided range of £17.2B - £17.6B. Except for this strengthened guidance, we reaffirm the outlook provided in our full year 2025 results.

data.title: Alignment Healthcare forecasts $138M-$163M adjusted EBITDA for 2026 as membership outlook rises to 294,000-299,000
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alignment Healthcare (ALHC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO John Kao said Q1 “health plan membership of 284,800 represented year-over-year membership growth of approximately 31%” and said the quarter produced “total revenue of $1.2 billion,” “adjusted gross profit of $146 million,” and “adjusted EBITDA was $38 million,” adding that this “exceeded the high end of our guidance range.”
  • Kao described a Q1 utilization disruption tied to CMS rules and internal workflow execution, saying, “the CMS rule change impacted our observation determination process” and that “this process change was resolved by the end of February, but impacted our first quarter inpatient admissions per 1,000, which was in the high 150s this quarter.”
  • Kao highlighted operational scaling initiatives, including, “just 12 months ago, our claims auto adjudication rate was less than 15%. Now our year-to-date auto adjudication rate is over 60%,” and said the company is “deploying contract management solutions that leverage AI” and “taking the next leap forward in our AVA AI risk stratification models.”
  • CFO James Head said Q1 results included “revenue of $1.2 billion,” “adjusted gross profit of $146 million represented an MBR of 88.2%,” and “adjusted EBITDA of $38 million produced an adjusted EBITDA margin of 3.1%,” while noting “GAAP SG&A was $121 million” and “adjusted SG&A was $108 million.”

Outlook

  • CFO James Head guided Q2 revenue of $1.30B-$1.32B vs. analysts’ $1.31223527B, and full-year 2026 revenue of $5.16B-$5.21B (the transcript did not provide EPS guidance).
  • Head raised full-year membership guidance to 294,000-299,000 and said, “we are increasing our membership growth expectation given continued strength within our sales operations and outperformance in member retention through the open enrollment period.”
  • Head lifted the low end of profitability ranges, stating, “we are raising the low end of each of our adjusted gross profit and adjusted EBITDA guidance ranges by $5 million,” and said the model assumes “inpatient admissions per 1,000 will run higher year-over-year” due to “growth amongst high acuity populations.”

Financial Results

  • Q1 revenue was $1.2B vs. analysts’ $1.21765528B (the transcript did not provide Q1 EPS).
  • CFO James Head said Q1 adjusted SG&A ratio improvement “outperformed the midpoint of our implied guidance range by 50 basis points” as “adjusted SG&A as a percentage of revenue declined…to 8.7%.”
  • Head reported liquidity of “$726 million in cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments” and said “the funded leverage ratio at the end of Q1 improved to 2.6x trailing 12-month EBITDA.”

Q&A

  • Matthew Gillmor, KeyBanc: Asked what changed in the hospital observation issue and post-fix trends; Founder, CEO John Kao: “we paid authorizations at full acute rates when we should have paid them at observation rates…It was a workflow problem,” adding it “impacted our January numbers, a couple of million dollars”; CFO Head: “Absent that issue…we probably in the mid-150s…The flu…was pretty much in line…we see that in April as well.”
  • John Stansel, JPMorgan: Asked about Q2 MBR improvement assumptions; CEO Kao: “second quarter is usually our seasonal better quarter,” and said first-half expectations include “MBR first half, 40 basis points…SG&A, 40 basis points…EBITDA is 90 basis points to 100 basis points.”
  • Scott Fidel, Goldman Sachs: Pressed for details, scope by market, and clawbacks; CEO Kao: “the answer is unfortunately no” on clawbacks and tied the issue to “dedelegating certain IPAs”; CFO Head: “by the end of January, we saw a little bit of an anomaly…by February, we had identified it and already corrected it.”
  • Benjamin Mayo, Leerink: Asked about risk adjustment on medically complex growth; CFO Head: “as it pertains to risk adjustment on the new members…we take a cautious stance,” adding “we book to the paid MMR.”
  • Jessica Tassan, Piper Sandler: Asked about the GLP-1 “bridge model” pilot; CEO Kao: “we would participate with certain conditions,” but said it “gets into…our product strategy for the ’27 bids, which I’d like to not discuss at this point.”
  • Andrew Mok, Barclays: Asked why lean into PPO and whether it becomes a bigger growth driver; CFO Head: “I don’t think we have figured out the secret sauce yet,” adding, “the unit economics are going to be pretty tough for people.”
  • Craig Jones, BofA: Asked how to handle a 2.48% net rate context vs trend; CEO Kao: “No way trend is going to be at 2.48%,” and said “L.A. County’s rate increases are closer to 6%.”
  • Ryan Daniels, William Blair: Asked about bringing supplemental benefits in-house; CEO Kao: “it’s an opportunity for us to lower MLR by bringing some of that in-house,” and said it is “not something we’re embedding into any of our thinking for the first half guidance.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on the utilization workflow disruption, MBR trajectory, rates/trend mismatch, and reserving; questions included “give us some sense for how hospital utilization trended,” and “is there an opportunity for you to claw back.”
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive/clarifying in Q&A on utilization, emphasizing scope and fixes; CEO Kao said, “It’s not a utilization issue,” and CFO Head repeatedly framed it as “a onetime course correction” and “corrective action.”
  • Versus last quarter, management in Q1 used more operational-detail explanations (workflow, de-delegation, claims automation) while maintaining confidence language; CEO Kao said, “I feel really good about this year, guys. I really do,” compared with Q4’s emphasis on multi-year profitability transformation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 introduced a discrete operational disruption (observation vs. acute authorization workflow) and described remediation timelines; Q4 did not cite a comparable internal process issue and instead emphasized navigating “the second phase of the V28 risk model” and “a redesign of the Part D program.”
  • Guidance changed directionally upward on membership and revenue in Q1; CFO Head said, “we are increasing our membership growth expectation,” and “raising our full year revenue guidance,” compared with Q4’s initial 2026 ranges.
  • Q1 analyst focus concentrated on utilization mechanics, MBR seasonality, and market expansion constraints, while Q4 questions leaned more toward rate-policy scenarios, AI use cases, and new-state provider engagement readiness.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged policy and operational execution risk, including “the CMS rule change” affecting utilization management workflows and the need to scale de-delegated functions as the company grows “outside of California.”
  • CEO Kao highlighted rate/trend tension, stating “No way trend is going to be at 2.48%,” and framed mitigation through market-level positioning, including “L.A. County’s rate increases are closer to 6%.”
  • CFO Head emphasized uncertainty around new-member risk adjustment timing, saying the company is “very cautious” and waits for “paid files” before recognizing potential upside.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a quarter of faster growth and stronger profitability than expected, while calling out a corrected, one-month utilization management workflow issue tied to observation determinations. Leadership raised 2026 membership and revenue outlook and lifted the low end of adjusted gross profit and adjusted EBITDA guidance, while reiterating that growth is increasingly oriented toward higher-acuity populations and supported by scaling initiatives such as claims automation and AI-enabled workflows. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly pressed on utilization, rate adequacy, and product/market expansion, and management responded by emphasizing rapid detection and remediation, cautious risk-adjustment recognition for new members, and continued plans to expand into new markets in 2027 without detailing bid tactics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cal Water expects $91M 2026 revenue growth in revised California rate case decision while targeting Nexus close by year-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: California Water Service Group (CWT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Q1 results were in line with our expectations, given the fact that we had a delayed 2024 general rate case," said "Chairman, President & CEO Martin Kropelnicki," adding, "yesterday, we received what's called a revised proposed decision" and "the revised proposed decision we're very happy with, and we are on the docket today for approval at the California Public Utilities Commission."
  • Kropelnicki highlighted capital activity and PFAS: "the highlight of the quarter is the fact that our infrastructure investment for the first quarter was up 17%," and said the company continues "progress on our PFAS treatment and cost recovery from the polluters."
  • "Senior VP, CFO, Principal Financial Officer & Treasurer James Lynch" said, "our first quarter results do not include the impact of the revenue requirement or any of the other provisions included in the revised proposed decision," and added, "the company does have an interim rates memorandum account, and that does authorize us to retroactively apply the decision back to January 1 once it's finalized."
  • Lynch reported, "In Q1 of 2026, revenue was $214.6 million compared to $204 million in the first quarter of 2025" and "Net income for the quarter was $4 million or $0.07 per diluted share, compared to the prior year's first quarter of $13.3 million or $0.22 per diluted share."
  • "Vice President of Rates & Regulatory Affairs Greg Milleman" described the rate case decision framework: "The revised proposed decision provides clear visibility into revenue growth, including approximately $91 million in 2026, followed by $43 million in 2027 and $49 million in 2028," and said it "authorizes cost-balancing accounts" and "introduces a new sales reconciliation mechanism and an updated rate design."

Outlook

  • Milleman said, "a final decision is expected later today or shortly thereafter," and Kropelnicki stated, "we are on the docket today for approval at the California Public Utilities Commission."
  • Kropelnicki said implementation would follow approval: "We are assuming an approval today, and we'd anticipate starting billing the new tariffs on July 1 of this year," while also noting, "there's a retroactive piece that goes back to January 1."
  • On acquisitions, Kropelnicki said of Nexus, "we could drive to close these transactions as early as the end of the year," and Lynch added, "We believe when the transaction is closed, it would likely occur towards the end of the year."

Financial Results

  • Lynch said Q1 earnings were shaped by multiple items: "The primary earnings drivers were rate increases, which added $0.11 per diluted share, and accrued and unbilled revenue, which added $0.06 per diluted share," while "increased depreciation and interest expense" and "a reduction in tax credits" were among offsets.
  • Lynch said capital spending accelerated: "our capital investments for the quarter were up 17.6% to $129.5 million," and added, "Our total planned capital investments for 2026 are $627 million."
  • On longer-term growth sensitivity to approvals, Lynch said, "If approved as requested... would result in a compounded annual rate base growth of over 11%."
  • Liquidity and financing capacity were framed as supportive of both capex and acquisitions: "As of March 31, 2026, we had $58.1 million in unrestricted cash and $45.6 million in restricted cash, along with approximately $470 million available on our bank lines of credit," and "we have over $340 million remaining on the shelf registration... after completing approximately $6.1 million of program sales during the first quarter."
  • Lynch reiterated shareholder return actions: "We just declared our 325th consecutive quarterly dividend of $0.335 per share" and "announced our 2026 annual dividend of $1.34 per share."

Q&A

  • Davis Sunderland, Baird: asked about potential EPA microplastics regulation and whether it could require incremental capex; Chairman, President & CEO Kropelnicki replied, "microplastics have shown up" on the UCMR list and "you will likely see regulations establishing MCL," while CFO Lynch added, "it's uncertain or unclear right now whether or not our current treatment that we're putting in place for PFAS will be effective for the microplastics."
  • Sunderland, Baird: asked about equity issuance and capital needs; CFO Lynch said funding will be timed around deal closes and noted "pretty interesting instruments out there relative to forwards" to "minimize any sort of dilution," while also stating, "Otherwise, we would continue to rely on our ATM and our normal lines of credit."
  • Sunderland, Baird: asked about PFAS cost recovery and funding; Kropelnicki said, "just last week, we received another $6.5 million gross" and "we have recovered about $66.5 million in gross receipts," while Lynch provided program framing: "our objective to get the treatment in by the end of 2028" and "about $60 million of that is for the wells, and the remainder is for treatment."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral and detail-oriented, focusing on potential new regulation, financing mechanics, and PFAS recoveries, including, "talk a bit about how you're thinking about equity issuance and capital needs" (Davis Sunderland, Baird).
  • Management tone was confident around the California rate case trajectory and M&A timing, including "we're very happy with the revised proposed decision" (Chairman, President & CEO Kropelnicki) and "we feel very confident that we'll be successful in closing both BVRT and the Nexus acquisitions" (CFO Lynch).
  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from expecting a proposed decision "very soon" to describing a "revised proposed decision" already received and "on the docket" for approval, tightening near-term visibility around the California GRC process.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Regulatory posture advanced from Q4’s expectation of a California proposed decision to Q1’s receipt of a "revised proposed decision" with explicit revenue growth figures of "approximately $91 million in 2026" and multiyear step-downs (Vice President Milleman).
  • The acquisition narrative moved from Q4’s announcement of the Nexus and BVRT agreements to Q1’s filings and timing discussion: "We filed our change of control applications" in Oregon and Nevada and "as early as the end of the year" for closing (Chairman, President & CEO Kropelnicki).
  • Leadership messaging introduced a personnel transition: "this is Greg's last earnings call with us" (Chairman, President & CEO Kropelnicki), a change not present in Q4.
  • Q4 prepared remarks emphasized storm-driven consumption impacts in December; Q1 emphasized interim rate case accounting and "warm and dry weather" affecting "accrued and unbilled revenue" (CFO Lynch).

Risks and Concerns

  • Kropelnicki flagged uncertainty around emerging contaminants: "you will likely see regulations establishing MCL" for microplastics, and CFO Lynch said it is "uncertain or unclear" whether PFAS treatment will address microplastics.
  • Capital and financing execution risk was linked to acquisitions and PFAS: Lynch said deal funding will be "incremental to our normal cadence," while Kropelnicki noted a PFAS-related commission application "will add further pressure on Jim on the capital side."
  • PFAS cost exposure was partially mitigated by recoveries: Kropelnicki said gross recoveries were "about $66.5 million," describing a "direct offset" to the PFAS program.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as an expectations-aligned quarter pending the California 2024 general rate case outcome, emphasizing that results exclude the revised proposed decision’s revenue requirement while retaining retroactive recovery through an interim mechanism. Executives highlighted rising infrastructure investment, a multiyear revenue growth framework embedded in the revised proposed decision, and continued progress on acquisitions in Nevada, Oregon, and Texas, alongside ongoing PFAS program buildout and litigation recoveries that management said are offsetting a meaningful portion of expected PFAS costs. They also indicated near-term operational focus on implementing new tariffs (including retroactive adjustments) and executing financing plans tied to transaction timing.
